name: Data patterns at scale
description: Outbox pattern and delivery guarantees, sharding and hot partitions, replication, optimistic locking and the N+1 problem — from the Dennemeyer system design 100-topic doc.

topics.name: Outbox pattern
topics.description: Solving the dual-write problem: persist state and publish events atomically via an outbox table.

topics.name: Sharding & partitioning
topics.description: Splitting data across nodes by key; choosing partition keys and handling hot shards.

topics.name: Replication & read replicas
topics.description: Copies of data for read scaling and availability; replication lag trade-offs.

topics.name: Optimistic locking
topics.description: Detecting concurrent updates with versions instead of holding locks.

questions.title: Outbox pattern and the dual write
questions.prompt: A service saves an order to its database and publishes an event to a queue. What can go wrong, and how does the outbox pattern fix it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Name the dual-write problem, write the event into an outbox table in the same transaction, relay it, and handle at-least-once on consumers.

expectedPoints.label: Names the dual-write problem
expectedPoints.description: DB commit and publish are two systems — one can succeed while the other fails, losing or ghosting events.

expectedPoints.label: Outbox row in the same transaction
expectedPoints.description: The event is inserted into an outbox table atomically with the business change; a relay publishes it later.

expectedPoints.label: Consumers handle at-least-once
expectedPoints.description: The relay may publish twice, so consumers deduplicate / are idempotent.

followUps.prompt: Does the outbox guarantee event ordering? What would you do if order matters?

questions.title: Sharding and hot partitions
questions.prompt: Your database no longer fits on one node, so you shard it. How do you pick the partition key, and what is a hot shard?
questions.answerStructureHint: Key must spread load evenly and match query patterns; hot shards come from skewed keys; mitigation via key salting or splitting.

expectedPoints.label: Partition key drives distribution and queries
expectedPoints.description: Pick a high-cardinality key that spreads writes and keeps common queries on one shard.

expectedPoints.label: Explains hot shards
expectedPoints.description: Skewed access (one big tenant, celebrity user, time-ordered keys) overloads one shard while others idle.

expectedPoints.label: Knows mitigations and costs
expectedPoints.description: Salting/composite keys, splitting shards, caching hot data; cross-shard transactions and rebalancing get hard.

followUps.prompt: You need to double the shard count on a live system. How do you migrate without downtime?

questions.title: Read replicas and replication lag
questions.prompt: You add read replicas to take load off the primary database. What new problems did you just introduce?
questions.answerStructureHint: Replication lag → stale reads and read-your-own-writes; routing decisions; failover consistency.

expectedPoints.label: Replication lag causes stale reads
expectedPoints.description: Replicas apply changes asynchronously, so reads can return old data.

expectedPoints.label: Read-your-own-writes problem
expectedPoints.description: A user updates then reads a replica and sees the old value; route their reads to the primary or use session stickiness.
acceptedSignals: sees the old value after saving
read from the leader after write

expectedPoints.label: Failover and lost writes
expectedPoints.description: Promoting a lagging replica on failover can lose recent writes; sync vs async is a durability/latency trade-off.

followUps.prompt: A user saves their profile and immediately sees the old name. What happened and how do you fix it?

questions.title: Optimistic locking and N+1
questions.prompt: Two users edit the same record at once. How does optimistic locking handle it — and separately, what is the N+1 query problem?
questions.answerStructureHint: Version column checked on update, conflict → retry/merge; N+1: one query per row from lazy loading, fixed with joins/batching.

expectedPoints.label: Version check on update
expectedPoints.description: Update carries the version it read; if the row changed meanwhile, the update fails instead of silently overwriting.

expectedPoints.label: Handles the conflict
expectedPoints.description: On conflict: reload and retry, merge, or surface to the user — better than blocking for rare conflicts.

expectedPoints.label: Explains N+1 and the fix
expectedPoints.description: One query for the list plus one per item (lazy loading); fix with joins, eager loading or batching.

followUps.prompt: How would you notice an N+1 problem in production rather than in code review?
